title: A book that surprised me
summary: Name a book and explain how it surprised you.
prompt: Write 150–250 words about a book that surprised you. Name the book, say how you got it, and explain why it was different from what you expected. Use past simple and 'I think / I feel' for opinions.
hintStarters: I did not want to read this book at first because…
sampleText: A friend in Lisbon lent me a book called The Year of the Hare by a Finnish writer. I did not want to read it at first because the cover was old and the title sounded boring. I expected a slow story about animals in a forest. I left the book on my desk for two weeks. One rainy Saturday, I had nothing to do, so I opened the first page. After two chapters, I could not stop. The book is about a tired journalist who hits a hare with his car and then leaves his whole city life to walk into the countryside with the small animal. It sounds strange, but the writing is funny and quiet at the same time. I laughed on the bus, and people looked at me. What surprised me most was the main character. He was not brave or special. He was just a normal, tired man, like many people I know. I think the book is really about choosing a smaller, slower life. I felt calmer after each chapter. I finished it in three days and gave it back to my friend with a long note inside. Now I never judge a book by its cover. Sometimes the quiet ones are the loudest in your head.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: cho tôi mượn một quyển sách

sampleAnnotations.phrase: judge a book by its cover
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đánh giá quyển sách qua cái bìa

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past simple for narrative
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I did not want to read it', 'I expected a slow story', 'I finished it in three days' — past simple kể chuỗi sự việc đã xảy ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I did not want to read it', 'I expected a slow story', 'I finished it in three days'. Past simple narrates a chain of finished events.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Opinion: 'I think' + clause
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I think the book is really about choosing a smaller, slower life.' — Dùng 'I think' + mệnh đề present simple để nêu ý kiến.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I think the book is really about choosing a smaller, slower life.' Use 'I think' + present simple clause for an opinion.

sampleTranslationVi: Một người bạn ở Lisbon cho tôi mượn quyển sách tên The Year of the Hare của một nhà văn Phần Lan. Lúc đầu tôi không muốn đọc vì cái bìa cũ và tựa đề nghe nhàm chán. Tôi tưởng đó là câu chuyện chậm về động vật trong rừng. Tôi để quyển sách trên bàn hai tuần. Một thứ Bảy mưa, tôi không có gì làm, nên mở trang đầu. Sau hai chương, tôi không thể dừng lại. Sách kể về một nhà báo mệt mỏi tông phải một con thỏ rừng rồi bỏ cả cuộc sống thành thị để đi bộ vào miền quê cùng con vật nhỏ. Nghe lạ, nhưng văn vừa hài hước vừa trầm lắng. Tôi cười trên xe buýt và người ta nhìn tôi. Điều khiến tôi ngạc nhiên nhất là nhân vật chính. Anh ấy không dũng cảm hay đặc biệt. Chỉ là một người đàn ông bình thường, mệt mỏi, giống nhiều người tôi biết. Tôi nghĩ cuốn sách thực ra nói về việc chọn một cuộc sống nhỏ hơn, chậm hơn. Tôi thấy bình tĩnh hơn sau mỗi chương. Tôi đọc xong trong ba ngày và trả lại cho bạn cùng một lời nhắn dài kẹp bên trong. Giờ tôi không bao giờ đánh giá quyển sách qua cái bìa. Đôi khi những quyển trầm lặng lại vang vọng nhất trong đầu bạn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct past tense of 'lend'.
options: A friend in Lisbon lended me a book.
A friend in Lisbon lent me a book.
A friend in Lisbon lend me a book.
A friend in Lisbon has lend me a book.
I did not want to read it but the cover was old.
I did not want to read it so the cover was old.
I did not want to read it because the cover was old.
I did not want to read it while the cover was old.
I am think the book is really about choosing a smaller life.
I think the book be really about choosing a smaller life.
I think the book really about choosing a smaller life.
I think the book is really about choosing a smaller life.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'I think' + subject + verb clause is the standard pattern.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the comparative correctly?
options: I felt calmer after each chapter.
I felt more calmer after each chapter.
I felt calm than after each chapter.
I felt the calmest after each chapter.
What surprise me most was the main character.
What surprised me most was the main character.
What surprised me most were the main character.
What surprised me the most main character was.
It sounds strange, so the writing is funny and quiet.
It sounds strange, because the writing is funny and quiet.
It sounds strange, but the writing is funny and quiet.
It sounds strange, while the writing is funny and quiet.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'But' signals contrast between 'strange' and 'funny and quiet'.

options: I finished it on three days.
I finished it at three days.
I finished it for three days.
I finished it in three days.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In + period of time' shows how long something took.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition fits a completed duration?

options: I left the book on my desk for two weeks.
I leaved the book on my desk for two weeks.
I leaving the book on my desk for two weeks.
I have left the book on my desk since two weeks.
Now I never judge a book for its cover.
Now I never judge a book by its cover.
Now I never judge a book on its cover.
Now I never judge a book with its cover.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Judge a book by its cover' is the fixed idiom.
mcQuestions.hint: It's a common idiom — what's the preposition?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which closing sentence is the most concrete?
options: In conclusion, books are powerful objects.
Throughout history, books have shaped lives.
Sometimes the quiet ones are the loudest in your head.
It is important to note that reading is good.
mcQuestions.explanation: A small specific paradox beats a generic AI-style line.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'In conclusion', 'Throughout history', or 'It is important to note'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever changed your opinion about something after just one book or one conversation? What was it?
